ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper proposes a concrete mechanism (an EM-style training rule that matches network output vectors to a target distribution using a weighting vector γ and pseudo-classes; Sec. 2.1) and a paired-MLP architecture where each network uses the other as target (Sec. 2.2). The authors themselves relate the idea to prior work (Sutskever et al.'s Output Distribution Matching and Siamese networks) and the method appears to be a non-trivial combination/extension of these ideas (mutual supervision + distribution matching + pseudo-labeling) rather than a wholly new paradigm. Overall this reads as a moderate/ incremental novelty: a specific, interesting training recipe for a classless pairing scenario but not a breakthrough new problem formulation or radically new method.

CLARITY.justification: The paper has a clear overall structure (Intro → Method → Experiments → Conclusion) and states its main contributions early with a concise bullet list (intro). The methodology section presents equations (1–5) and an EM-style E/M description, but some notation and procedural details are ambiguous (the element-wise power operation and the exact update schedule for pseudo-classes and γ are under-specified). Figures (e.g. Figure 4/5) are informative for training dynamics, but references to results (Table 1) are not shown in the provided text. Writing is generally readable but contains repeated paragraphs, several grammatical/typo issues (e.g. “distrubtion”, “stars at”), and occasional awkward phrasing that hinder smooth comprehension.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper presents empirical evidence (MNIST-derived experiments, Sec. 3–4) that the proposed dual-MLP self-labeling procedure outperforms k-means / agglomerative clustering and approaches a supervised MLP, but the mathematical treatment is informal and incomplete (Sec. 2.1): the ‘‘EM’’ formulation, the use of element-wise power on outputs, the absence of specification of output normalization/activation and the gradient derivation for γ are not justified. The experimental methodology omits variance/confidence reporting and stronger baselines (e.g. ODM/contrastive or other cross-modal alignment methods), uses an unusually large minibatch (25% of training) and leaves some update-scheduling details unspecified (Sec. 3), which limits reproducibility and generality. Overall the core claims are partially supported by experiments on synthetic MNIST variants, but there are methodological and theoretical gaps that warrant caution before accepting broader conclusions.
